elements.sent-2411-001: Image manipulation can lead to misinterpretation of visual content, posing significant risks to information security.
elements.sent-2411-002: Image Manipulation Localization (IML) has thus received increasing attention.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, existing IML methods rely heavily on task-specific designs, making them perform well only on one target image type but are mostly random guessing on other image types, and even joint training on multiple image types causes significant performance degradation.
elements.sent-2411-004: This hinders the deployment for real applications as it notably increases maintenance costs and the misclassification of image types leads to serious error accumulation.
elements.sent-2411-005: To this end, we propose Omni-IML, the first generalist model to unify diverse IML tasks.
elements.sent-2411-006: Specifically, Omni-IML achieves generalism by adopting the Modal Gate Encoder and the Dynamic Weight Decoder to adaptively determine the optimal encoding modality and the optimal decoder filters for each sample.
elements.sent-2411-007: We additionally propose an Anomaly Enhancement module that enhances the features of tampered regions with box supervision and helps the generalist model to extract common features across different IML tasks.
elements.sent-2411-008: We validate our approach on IML tasks across three major scenarios: natural images, document images, and face images.
elements.sent-2411-009: Without bells and whistles, our Omni-IML achieves state-of-the-art performance on all three tasks with a single unified model, providing valuable strategies and insights for real-world application and future research in generalist image forensics.
elements.sent-2411-010: Our code will be publicly available.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{teaser.pdf} \caption{The proposed Omni-IML is the first generalist model for image manipulation localization. It that can simultaneously achieve high performance forgery localization on natural images, document images and face images with a single model, without task-specific or benchmark-specific fine-tuning.} \label{fig: teaser}
elements.sent-2411-011: The rapid advancement of image processing software and deep generative models has considerably enriched human capability to create innovative visual content.
elements.sent-2411-012: Users can effortlessly manipulate the visual appearance and create new images that do not exist~\cite{qu2024generalizedtamperedscenetext .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Inevitably, such forged images can lead to fraud and the spread of rumors, posing significant risks to politics, economics, and personal privacy~\cite{CVPR2023DocTamper .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Consequently, Image Manipulation Localization (IML) has become an emerging issue in social media security~\cite{sun2023safl .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Despite the progress made in recent years, existing IML models are designed for individual image types (e.g. natural style images, document images, face images).
elements.sent-2411-016: Although these specialized models can handle multiple tampering methods on the images of a single target type, they always fall short on other types of forged images.
elements.sent-2411-017: The lack of generality notably increases the maintenance costs of IML, since an additional image type classifier and multiple IML models must be maintained for different image types.
elements.sent-2411-018: In addition, the error accumulation caused by image type misclassification is still severe, as the existing IML models perform poorly on the image types they are not designed for.
elements.sent-2411-019: This significantly hinders the real-world application of IML.
elements.sent-2411-020: It is crucial to develop a generalist IML model that can simultaneously perform well on all image types.
elements.sent-2411-021: Jointly training an IML model on diverse image types can slightly alleviate the random guessing issue on different image types.
elements.sent-2411-022: However, in most cases, the joint training will lead to an obvious performance degradation on all image types, making the predictions unreliable.
elements.sent-2411-023: For example, HiFi-Net~\cite{hifi_net_xiaoguo suffers from joint training and thus uses two different sets of model parameters for natural images and face images separately.}
elements.sent-2411-024: There are two main reasons why existing IML methods suffer so much from joint training: First, existing IML methods rely heavily on specific architecture designs, input modalities, and training strategies to detect specific tampering clues on specific image types.
elements.sent-2411-025: These designs work well for the target image type, but usually not so well for other image types.
elements.sent-2411-026: For example, edge anomaly enhancement modules~\cite{dong2022mvss and object attention modules~\cite{wang2022objectformer have made significant progress in identifying forged natural objects.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: However, they can hardly work well on document and face images where edge artifacts are not obvious.
elements.sent-2411-028: Early frequency-vision~\cite{CVPR2023DocTamper fusion achieves satisfactory performance on document images but has obvious performance degradation on natural and face images that cover much more noise and diversity.}
elements.sent-2411-029: The high-resolution representation learning design with shallow layers~\cite{hifi_net_xiaoguo, liu2022pscc performs well in capturing the texture anomalies left by deepfake models but falls short on natural and document images where the tampered regions are small and the texture anomalies are not obvious.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Second, existing IML methods lack the design to alleviate the confusion in unified IML modeling.
elements.sent-2411-031: The IML task is already challenging since various tampering methods have already produced different unobvious tampering cues on each single image type, learning a general representation for tampering cues on different image types could be even more challenging.
elements.sent-2411-032: Without a suitable design, models will be easily confused when learning to distinguish so many tampering features from authentic ones.
elements.sent-2411-033: To address the above issues, we propose Omni-IML, the first generalist model that can simultaneously perform well on all three major IML tasks with a single model, as shown in Fig~\ref{fig: teaser .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Specifically, a Modal Gate Encoder is proposed to automatically select the optimal encoding modality for each input sample, based on the characteristics of the input image.
elements.sent-2411-035: Additionally, a Dynamic Weight Decoder is proposed to adaptively select the optimal decoder filters for each sample, assisting the generalist model to better cope with the highly diverse tampering features from different tampering methods on multiple image types.
elements.sent-2411-036: These sample-adaptive designs effectively help the model achieve generalism through flexibly adapting itself to each sample.
elements.sent-2411-037: Further, an Anomaly Enhancement module is introduced between the encoder and decoder.
elements.sent-2411-038: It enhances the features of tampered regions with a novel box supervision design and suppresses the noise introduced by the joint learning on different tampering methods and image types.
elements.sent-2411-039: We validate the effectiveness of our Omni-IML on three representative IML tasks, including natural IML, document IML, and face IML.
elements.sent-2411-040: Without bells and whistles, experimental results showcase that our single model achieves state-of-the-art performance simultaneously on all three tasks, significantly surpassing previous specialized methods on individual tasks.
elements.sent-2411-041: These strong results verify the design of our generalist model in the field of image forensics.
elements.sent-2411-042: By unifying the IML on natural images, document images and face images with a single model, our Omni-IML successfully eliminates the trouble of judging image type at first and maintaining different models for diverse image types.
elements.sent-2411-043: The issues of severe error accumulation and high maintenance costs are thus well solved, significantly promoting the real-world applications of IML.
elements.sent-2411-044: The development of Omni-IML is also in line with the current main trend towards Artificial General Intelligence (AGI).
elements.sent-2411-045: In summary, our main contributions are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-046: We propose Omni-IML, the first generalist model for image manipulation localization, which serves as a pioneering effort in this field.
elements.sent-2411-047: Our technical innovations lie in the novel and effective modules: (1) Modal Gate Encoder to effectively select sample-specific encoding modality, facilitating better modality collaboration.
elements.sent-2411-048: Extensive experiments demonstrate that our generalist model can simultaneously achieve state-of-the-art results with a single model on natural image IML, document IML and face IML.
elements.sent-2411-049: Natural Image Manipulation Localization aims to identify the tampered regions in daily-life style images.
elements.sent-2411-050: Mantra-Net~\cite{wu2019mantra proposes to perform natural IML with noise filters SRM and Bayar Conv.}
elements.sent-2411-051: MVSS-Net~\cite{dong2022mvss introduces ESB module to enhance boundary inconsistency.}
elements.sent-2411-052: ObjectFormer~\cite{wang2022objectformer proposes an object encoder to learn object-level attention for better feature extraction and proposes BSCIM module to enhance the edge inconsistency.}
elements.sent-2411-053: TruFor~\cite{guillaro2023trufor benefits from the noise filters Noiseprint++.}
elements.sent-2411-054: UnionFormer~\cite{Qu_2024_CVPR introduces a new backbone to enhance edge artifacts, and proposes to model the inconsistency between tampered objects and authentic objects.}
elements.sent-2411-055: These model designs have achieved significant progress in natural images, but their performance in document and face forensics scenarios is unsatisfactory due to the the absence of natural object, edge artifacts and noise artifacts in these scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-056: Document Image Manipulation Localization aims to localize the forged regions in document images.
elements.sent-2411-057: Early works~\cite{doc1, doc2 achieve document forensics through template-matching based methods.}
elements.sent-2411-058: These methods work well on clean documents but do not excel on complex documents such as photographed documents, and even cannot work on natural or face images.
elements.sent-2411-059: Document Tampering Detector~\cite{CVPR2023DocTamper improves document IML through early fusion of vision and frequency features.}
elements.sent-2411-060: However, the model will be seriously distorted in many cases of natural and face images where the frequency features are too noisy.
elements.sent-2411-061: TIFDM~\cite{dong2024robust proposes high-level spatial attention to suppress the false alarms in documents, but it is limited on complex natural images.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Face Image Manipulation Localization aims to localize fake human faces.
elements.sent-2411-063: The advancement of deepfake techniques makes it easy to generate a face that does not exist~\cite{deepfake1, fashifter .}
elements.sent-2411-064: To ensure the security of face images and improve the interpretability of deepfake detection, some recent works have explored face image forgery localization, characterized by a shallow network design for texture artifacts detection.
elements.sent-2411-065: HiFiNet~\cite{hifi_net_xiaoguo utilizes metric learning for better texture anomaly capturing.}
elements.sent-2411-066: DA-HFNet~\cite{liu2024hfnet proposes Dual Attention Feature Fusion to better capture the AIGC artifacts.}
elements.sent-2411-067: These methods show generalization on face IML but are sub-optimal on natural and document images, where the tampered regions are small in size and the visual anomalies are less obvious.
elements.sent-2411-068: Recently, generalist model has attracted increasing attention since it is more convenient for academic and application~\cite{wang2022ofa .}
elements.sent-2411-069: Despite the progress in unified object detection and segmentation~\cite{wang2023seggpt , most of the previous generalist models do not cover all image forensic tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-070: EVP~\cite{liu2023explicit unifies natural image forensics with other low-level tasks such as shadow detection, but it can only perform IML on natural images and its performance is not satisfactory enough.}
elements.sent-2411-071: Therefore, EVP cannot be considered as a generalist model for IML.
elements.sent-2411-072: For image forgery localization, none of the existing work realizes a unified model that can be simultaneously generalized to natural images, document images and face images.
elements.sent-2411-073: It is still unexplored towards a generalist IML model that can generalize on various tampering methods across different image types.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{main_fig.pdf} \caption{The overall framework of the proposed Omni-IML.} \label{fig: main}
elements.sent-2411-074: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig: main , the overall architecture of the proposed Omni-IML is roughly based on encoder-decoder architecture.}
elements.sent-2411-075: The Modal Gate Encoder of the Omni-IML consists of four modules: (1) Visual Perception Head (VPH) to extract visual features from the original images; (2) Frequency Perception Head (FPH) to convert the Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) coefficients of the images to frequency domain features; (3) a Modal Gate to automatically determine the optimal modality for the following encoding process; (4) a backbone model to extract multi-scale high-level features from the output of the Modal Gate.
elements.sent-2411-076: The Dynamic Weight Decoder of the Omni-IML adaptively selects the sample-specific optimal decoder filters and outputs the final mask prediction.
elements.sent-2411-077: We also design an Anomaly Enhancement module between the encoder and decoder, to enhance the common features of tampered regions from various image types.
elements.sent-2411-078: The frequency feature is a double-edged sword for the IML generalist.
elements.sent-2411-079: The frequency feature can help to detect visually consistent tampering in some cases, but it can also degrade the model performance when the image is complex and noisy, or the frequency information is not prominent in the original image.
elements.sent-2411-080: As a result, neither pure vision modeling nor vision+frequency modeling can consistently provide the optimal solution.
elements.sent-2411-081: In order to achieve general IML through a flexible encoding modality, we propose the Modal Gate, which automatically determines the optimal encoding modality (frequency+vision or pure vision) for each input sample.
elements.sent-2411-082: The key idea of our Modal Gate Encoder is to automatically identify the optimal modality by analyzing whether the frequency features contain too much noise, and which coarse prediction seems more confident, reliable, and accurate.. Image Encoding.
elements.sent-2411-083: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig: main , the Omni-IML considers both vision domain modeling and frequency domain modeling.}
elements.sent-2411-084: Given an input image $X \in$ $\mathbb{R ^{H \times W \times 3}$ and its Y-channel quantization table $QT \in$ $\mathbb{R ^{8 \times 8}$, we extract vision features $F_{rgb}$ using Visual Perception Head (VPH), $F_{rgb =VPH(X)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-085: We obtain frequency features $F_{freq}$ from the DCT coefficients and quantization tables (QT) of the images using Frequency Perception Head (FPH), $F_{freq =FPH(DCT(X), QT)$.}
elements.sent-2411-086: We use the same VPH and FPH architectures as those proposed in Document Tampering Detector~\cite{CVPR2023DocTamper .}
elements.sent-2411-087: The $F_{freq}$ is fused with $F_{rgb}$ by a channel-spatial attention module $Attn$ to get the fused features $F_{fused}$, $F_{fused =Attn(F_{rgb , F_{freq )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: Two coarse binary mask predictions $P_{rgb}$ and $P_{fused}$ are further obtained from $F_{rgb}$ and $F_{fused}$ with two auxiliary heads $AuxHead$ respectively, $P_{rgb =AuxHead_{1 (F_{rgb )$, $P_{fused =AuxHead_{2 (F_{fused )$, each of the auxiliary heads consists of two conv-layers.. Modal Gate.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-089: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig: MG , the input of the proposed Modal Gate has four parts: $F_{rgb}$, $F_{fused}$, $P_{rgb}$ and $P_{fused}$; We repeat $P_{rgb}$, $P_{fused}$ and concatenate them with $F_{rgb}$, $F_{fused}$ to get $F_{cat}$, which is then fed into a binary classifier for optimal modality prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-090: The classifier $CLS$ consists of several conv-layers, a global average pooling layer and a linear layer, and is used to determine whether to use the fused feature $F_{fused}$ or the pure vision feature as the encoder input $F_{rgb}$, by observing the noise level and anomaly significance level of $F_{fused}$, $F_{rgb}$ and their corresponding coarse predictions $P_{rgb}$ and $P_{fused}$.. Loss Function.
elements.sent-2411-091: The Modal Gate Encoder is optimized with $L_{MG}$, the sum of two segmentation losses and one classification loss.
elements.sent-2411-092: Our Modal Gate Encoder extracts the best features from different image types and thus considerably benefits the generalist IML model.
elements.sent-2411-093: Sophisticated tampering leaves very obscure anomaly clues.
elements.sent-2411-094: The encoder's output feature from such challenging sample can be very noisy.
elements.sent-2411-095: Different image types produce different features and thus joint training brings much more noise to the features and confuses IML model.
elements.sent-2411-096: To tackle this, we propose to enhance the features of forged regions and suppress the noise through including an extra box supervision during training.
elements.sent-2411-097: Since the detection framework has a clear different characteristic from the original segmentation one, training the model under both frameworks further highlights anomaly features by reducing the learning bias: If a feature region reports positive under both the detection and segmentation frameworks, it can mostly be the actual tampered region.
elements.sent-2411-098: However, if a feature region reports positive under only one framework, it is likely to be a false-positive noise and will be punished under the other framework.
elements.sent-2411-099: As a result, the contrast between the features of forged regions and authentic regions can be strengthened, noise can be suppressed and the common tampering features can be learned.
elements.sent-2411-100: However, directly training the model with the two frameworks may also cause task competition for model parameters~\cite{heuer2021multitask and weaken model performance, while directly scaling up the model parameters could alleviate the competition but will increase computation burden.}
elements.sent-2411-101: To address this issue, we propose a novel effective collaboration module Anomaly Enhancement (AE).. Method.
elements.sent-2411-102: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig: AE , for the input features $F_{2}$ and $F_{3}$, we first extract task-agnostic features $F_{a}$ and $F_{b}$ with query-based attention, the learnable attention queries contain prior knowledge to decouple and to minimize negative impact from the segmentation supervision.}
elements.sent-2411-103: After that, $F_{a}$ and $F_{b}$ are processed by the detection modules, including two Feature Pyramid Networks (FPNs) ~\cite{lin2017fpn and the Faster R-CNN's~\cite{ren2015fasterrcnn RPN and RoI-Heads.}}
elements.sent-2411-104: The detection modules (black arrows in Figure~\ref{fig: AE ) are only present during training.}
elements.sent-2411-105: Including the two cascaded FPNs reduces parameter competition from the detection framework and discarding them during inference ensures the computation efficiency, successfully addressing the dilemma.
elements.sent-2411-106: After training, the $F_{a}$ and $F_{b}$ contain positive features enhanced by the detection supervision, we add them to the original features $F_{2}$ and $F_{3}$ and fuse them with conv-layer to get $F_{2'}$ and $F_{3'}$.. Loss Function.
elements.sent-2411-107: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig: AE , the AE module is optimized by bounding box losses as Faster R-CNN~\cite{ren2015fasterrcnn from the RPN and RoI-Head.}}
elements.sent-2411-108: The ground-truth boxes are the bounding boxes of the mask labels' connected regions.
elements.sent-2411-109: The AE module is tested in an end2end manner as shown in Figure~\ref{fig: AE .}
elements.sent-2411-110: The proposed AE effectively achieves task collaboration while keeping the inference cost almost unchanged.
elements.sent-2411-111: With the proposed AE module, the tampered regions in features $F_{2}$ and $F_{3}$ can be enhanced and the false-positive noise can be reduced.
elements.sent-2411-112: Consequently, our AE module helps to extract better common features and thus benefits the generalist model.
elements.sent-2411-113: Different types of tampered image result in a wide range of manipulation clues.
elements.sent-2411-114: For example, forged objects in natural style images may have abnormal contrast or edge artifacts~\cite{wang2022objectformer , tampered text in document images might be visually consistent but has discontinuous BAG in frequency domain~\cite{CVPR2023DocTamper , fake faces may have unnatural texture~\cite{hifi_net_xiaoguo .}}}
elements.sent-2411-115: These wide variations of tampering clues further cause a large variation of the encoded features of tampered regions.
elements.sent-2411-116: Merely using a fixed set of filters for the decoder causes it being confused by the diverse encoder features, especially in the unified training process.
elements.sent-2411-117: To address this challenge, we propose to adaptively select the optimal decoder filters for each input image based on the characteristics of the image and the initial predicted tampered region.
elements.sent-2411-118: To achieve this, we propose the DWD, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig: DWD .. Method.}
elements.sent-2411-119: In the proposed Dynamic Weight Decoder, the low-level input features are fused with high-level input features by Pyramid Pooling Module ~\cite{zhao2017pyramid and Feature Pyramid Network~\cite{lin2017fpn to obtain multi scale features $F_{1 , F_{2 , F_{3 , F_{4}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-120: A global feature vector $V_{g}$ is obtained by average pooling $F_{4}$.
elements.sent-2411-121: A coarse mask prediction $P_{co}$ is obtained from the lowest-level feature $F_{1}$ by a conv-layer, $P_{co =Conv(F_{1 )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-122: A light-weight network $CNN$ is used to extract features $F_{co}$ from the coarse prediction $P_{co}$, $F_{co =CNN(P_{co )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-123: The extracted feature is concatenated to the multi-scale features and it helps the model to pay attention to the suspicious regions and analyze the forgery type, $F_{cat =Concat(F_{1 , F_{2 , F_{3 , F_{4 , F_{co )$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-124: The concatenated features are channel dimension reduced and processed by a series of Dynamic Weight Filters (DWF) with different dilation rates, $F_{dec1 =Concat(Avg(F_{cat ), F_{dw , F_{cat )$, $F_{dw =Concat([DWF_n(F_{cat , V_{g ) \; for \;n \;in \;(2,3,6)])$, $DWF_n$ denotes the proposed DWF with dilation rate $n$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-125: The final prediction $P_{DWD}$ is obtained by $P_{DWD =Conv(DWD_2(DWD_2(Conv(F_{dec1 ),V_g),V_g))$,~where $Conv$ denotes $1\times1$ conv-layer.}}
elements.sent-2411-126: The DWD is surprised by minimizing the cross-entropy loss between $P_{DWD}$, $P_{co}$ and the ground-truth mask $L_m$.
elements.sent-2411-127: As shown in the top-right of Figure~\ref{fig: DWD , to obtain the dynamic filters, we first average pool the input feature to obtain a current global representation $V_c$ (orange box in Figure~\ref{fig: DWD ), then interact $V_c$ with the global image vector $V_g$ (blue box in Figure~\ref{fig: DWD ) with a fully connected layer and identify the optimal dynamic filters $D_{opt}$ by weighted summation of four common convolutional filters.}}}
elements.sent-2411-128: Finally, we depth-wise convolve the input feature with $D_{opt}$ and then perform point-wise convolution with $1 \times 1$ conv-layer to obtain the output.
elements.sent-2411-129: The proposed DWD achieves sample-specific filters selection by analyzing the characteristics of the input image, the input features and the forgery types in the initially predicted tampered region.
elements.sent-2411-130: The selected optimal filters effectively help the generalist model to simultaneously distinguish tampered regions in different image types.

elements.sent-2411-191: In this paper, we propose Omni-IML, the first generalist model designed for image manipulation localization to address the drawbacks of specialist models.
elements.sent-2411-192: Specifically, multiple novel and effective modules are proposed to achieve generalism through sample-specific adaptation, including a Modal Gate Encoder that automatically determines the optimal encoding modality for each input image, and a Dynamic Weight Decoder that adaptively selects the optimal decoder parameters for each input sample.
elements.sent-2411-193: In addition, an Anomaly Enhancement module is proposed to reduce confusion by enhancing the features of tampered regions and driving the model to learn common features from diverse image types.
elements.sent-2411-194: To verify the generalist capability, extensive experiments are conducted on three major IML tasks, covering natural IML, document IML, and face IML.
elements.sent-2411-195: The experimental results demonstrate that our single model simultaneously achieves state-of-the-art performance on all tasks.
elements.sent-2411-196: Comprehensive ablation studies and visual analyses are also presented to provide in-depth insights.
elements.sent-2411-197: We believe that our work can inspire future research and promote the real-world applications of unified image forensics models.. { \bibliographystyle{ieeenat_fullname.}}
elements.sent-2411-198: Omni-IML: Towards Unified Image Manipulation Localization \city{Guangzhou \country{China \city{Hong Kong \country{China \city{Shanghai \country{China \city{Guangzhou \country{China.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-199: Existing Image Manipulation Localization (IML) methods mostly rely heavily on task-specific designs, making them perform well only on the target IML task, while joint training on multiple IML tasks causes significant performance degradation, hindering real applications.
elements.sent-2411-200: To this end, we propose Omni-IML, the first generalist model designed to unify IML across diverse tasks.
elements.sent-2411-201: Specifically, Omni-IML achieves generalization through three key components: (1) a Modal Gate Encoder, which adaptively selects the optimal encoding modality per sample, (2) a Dynamic Weight Decoder, which dynamically adjusts decoder filters to the task at hand, and (3) an Anomaly Enhancement module that leverages box supervision to highlight the tampered regions and facilitate the learning of task-agnostic features.
elements.sent-2411-202: Beyond localization, to support interpretation of the tampered images, we construct Omni-273k, a large high-quality dataset that includes natural language descriptions of tampered artifact.
elements.sent-2411-203: It is annotated through our automatic, chain-of-thoughts annotation technique.
elements.sent-2411-204: We also design a simple-yet-effective interpretation module to better utilize these descriptive annotations.
elements.sent-2411-205: Our extensive experiments show that our single Omni-IML model achieves state-of-the-art performance across all four major IML tasks, providing a valuable solution for practical deployment and a promising direction of generalist models in image forensics.
elements.sent-2411-206: Our code and dataset will be publicly available.
elements.sent-2411-207: The rapid advancement of image processing technique makes it easy to manipulate images, incurring risks in social media security~\cite{sun2023safl .}
elements.sent-2411-208: Despite the progress made in recent years, existing IML models are designed for individual IML tasks on specific image types (e.g., natural image or document etc.) and usually fall short on other IML tasks.
elements.sent-2411-209: Consequently, the maintenance costs are high since every IML task requires an independent, task-specific IML model.
elements.sent-2411-210: A naive solution is to jointly train an IML model using the data of all available IML tasks.
elements.sent-2411-211: However, joint training usually leads to an obvious performance degradation on all IML tasks, making the predictions unreliable.
elements.sent-2411-212: For example, HiFi-Net~\cite{hifi_net_xiaoguo suffers from joint training and thus uses two different sets of model parameters for natural image IML and face IML, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-213: There are two main reasons why existing IML methods still cannot provide generalization across IML tasks after joint training: First, existing IML methods heavily rely on task-dependent architecture designs, input modalities, and training strategies to detect tampering clues.
elements.sent-2411-214: These designs work well for the target IML task, but usually fall short on other IML tasks.
elements.sent-2411-215: For example, edge anomaly enhancement modules~\cite{dong2022mvss, Yu_2024_CVPR and object attention modules~\cite{wang2022objectformer, Li_2024_CVPR have made significant progress in identifying forged natural objects.}}
elements.sent-2411-216: However, they can hardly work well on document images where edge artifacts are less obvious and object features are not distinct.
elements.sent-2411-217: Early frequency-vision~\cite{CVPR2023DocTamper fusion performs well on document images but has obvious performance degradation on natural images that cover much more noise and diversity.}
elements.sent-2411-218: Second, existing IML methods lack the design to distinguish diverse tampering features across different IML tasks.
elements.sent-2411-219: The IML task is challenging since the tampering methods are diverse and produce different subtle tampering cues.
elements.sent-2411-220: Further, it is even more challenging to handle various IML tasks using a unified model.
elements.figure-2411-014: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{teaser.pdf} \caption{The proposed Omni-IML is the first generalist model for IML. A single model can simultaneously achieve state-of-the-art performance on multiple major IML tasks, without task-specific and benchmark-specific fine-tuning.} \label{fig: teaser}
elements.sent-2411-221: To address the above issues, we propose Omni-IML, the first generalist model that can simultaneously perform well on multiple major IML tasks, as shown in Fig~\ref{fig: teaser .}
elements.sent-2411-222: And a Dynamic Weight Decoder is proposed to adaptively select the optimal decoder filters for each sample, assisting the generalist model to better cope with the highly diverse tampering features from multiple image types.
elements.sent-2411-223: These sample-adaptive designs can provide flexibility so that the model can adapt to each input sample and achieve generalization.
elements.sent-2411-224: Further, an Anomaly Enhancement module is introduced to enhance the features of tampered regions with a novel box supervision design.
elements.sent-2411-225: Merely localizing tampered regions is not convincing enough~\cite{xu2024fakeshield, huang2024ffaa .}
elements.sent-2411-226: We also seek to improve model reliability by enabling the language description on both visual and semantic artifacts of the tampered image.
elements.sent-2411-227: Despite the recent progress in interpretable IML on natural images, there still lacks public data for interpretable IML on documents, scene text images and uncut deepfake images.
elements.sent-2411-228: The interpretable IML annotation generation pipelines with GPT-4o in previous works~\cite{xu2024fakeshield, huang2024sida work well on natural images, but do not work well on documents, uncut deepfakes and scene texts, where a tampered image usually contains multiple tampered regions and the image artifact are less obvious~\cite{CVPR2023DocTamper .}}
elements.sent-2411-229: There are two main reasons behind this gap: First, previous methods obtain the content, position and artifact descriptions of the tampered object with only a single query to GPT-4o.
elements.sent-2411-230: When there are multiple tampered objects in one image, the GPT-4o is easily distracted and messes up the descriptions among different targets.
elements.sent-2411-231: Second, when the image artifact is less obvious, the GPT-4o's response will be less confident and even incorrect, resulting in low-quality annotations.
elements.sent-2411-232: To this end, we propose a novel chain-of-thoughts pipeline, which solves the above annotation issues through step-by-step focused analysis and self-examination.
elements.sent-2411-233: Specifically, we first recognize all the tampered objects and their locations.
elements.sent-2411-234: For each tampered object, we query GPT-4o to generate its artifact description with an elaborate prompt, and then query GPT-4o to examine its previous response.
elements.sent-2411-235: With the proposed method, we generate artifact descriptions on forged natural images, documents, faces, scene texts, and construct a large-scale, comprehensive, high quality dataset Omni-273k.
elements.sent-2411-236: To make better use of our Omni-273k, we further propose a simple-yet-effective interpretation module that improves model's artifact description performance through a reference visual prompt.
elements.sent-2411-237: Due to limited computational resources, it is not practical to validate our model on all IML tasks and datasets in the world.
elements.sent-2411-238: Instead, we validated our methods on hundreds of representative IML tasks (e.g., IML on certificates, product photos, artwork, street photos, cards, signs, group photos, receipts, etc.), which can be categorized into four distinct major IML tasks, including natural image IML, document IML, face IML and scene text IML, covering the vast majority of the recent IML research.
elements.sent-2411-239: Extensive experiments on the four major IML tasks show that joint training the existing IML methods on all tasks leads to significant performance degradation and the trained IML models are inadequate to handle multiple IML tasks simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-240: While our Omni-IML can minimize the performance degradation and simultaneously achieve state-of-the-art performance across all the four major IML tasks, demonstrating high scalability and effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-241: We propose Omni-IML, the first IML generalist model that enables generalizable image forgery localization and interpretation, serving as a pioneering effort in the field.
elements.sent-2411-242: Unified IML modeling is achieved by multliple novel and effective modules, consisting of Modal Gate Encoder, Anomaly Enhancement, and Dynamic Weight Decoder.
elements.sent-2411-243: Further, to interpret image artifact in natural language, we propose a novel chain-of-thoughts annotation technique to automatically construct Omni-273k, a large-scale and high-quality dataset.
elements.sent-2411-244: A simple-yet-effective interpretation module is also proposed to better leverage our data.
elements.sent-2411-245: Extensive experiments demonstrate that our single generalist model can simultaneously achieve state-of-the-art results across different major IML tasks.
elements.sent-2411-246: Natural IML aims to identify the tampered regions in daily-life style images.
elements.sent-2411-247: MVSS-Net~\cite{dong2022mvss introduces ESB module to enhance edge inconsistency.}
elements.sent-2411-248: ObjectFormer~\cite{wang2022objectformer proposes an object encoder to learn object-level attention for better feature extraction.}
elements.sent-2411-249: Trufor~\cite{guillaro2023trufor benefits from the Bayar noise filters.}
elements.sent-2411-250: These models perform well on natural images, but mostly not well enough on other image types (e.g. documents), due to the the absence of natural object, edge artifact and noise artifact in these scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-251: FakeShield~\cite{xu2024fakeshield improves natural IML based on LISA~\cite{lai2024lisa , which is constrained in multi-target scenarios~\cite{xia2024gsva and is thus challenged to achieve reliable IML on text images and uncut deepfake images~\cite{le2021openforensics .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-252: Document IML aims to localize the forged regions in document images.
elements.sent-2411-253: These methods work well on clean documents but struggle with complex images such as photographed documents.
elements.sent-2411-254: DTD~\cite{CVPR2023DocTamper improves document IML through early fusion of vision and frequency features.}
elements.sent-2411-255: Face IML aims to localize AI-generated fake faces.
elements.sent-2411-256: MoNFAP~\cite{liu2024hfnet improves texture anomaly detection with a set of noise filters.}
elements.sent-2411-257: These methods show generalization on face IML but are suboptimal on natural and document images, where the tampered regions are small in size and the texture anomalies are less obvious.
elements.sent-2411-258: Scene Text IML aims to localize tampered natural scene text in arbitrary styles and complex backgrounds.
elements.sent-2411-259: Previous works relies on specific noise patterns~\cite{wang2022tic or pre-training on authentic scene text images, limiting their generalization across other IML tasks.}

elements.sent-2411-260: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig: main , the Omni-IML has a localization module to identify whether the input image is tampered with and to localize the tampered region, and an interpretation module to describe the artifact for tampered image in natural language.}
elements.sent-2411-261: The localization module is roughly based on an encoder-decoder architecture.
elements.sent-2411-262: The Modal Gate Encoder consists of: (1) Visual Perception Head to extract visual features from the original images; (2) Frequency Perception Head to extract frequency features; (3) a Modal Gate to automatically determine the optimal modality for the following encoding process; (4) a backbone model to extract multi-scale high-level features.
elements.sent-2411-263: The Dynamic Weight Decoder adaptively selects the sample-wise optimal decoder filters and outputs the final mask prediction.
elements.sent-2411-264: The interpretation module is a multimodal LLM, it describes the image artifact by examining both the original input image and a reference visual prompt constructed by highlighting the decoder's mask prediction on the original input image.
elements.sent-2411-265: The frequency feature is a double-edged sword for the IML generalist: it can help to detect visually consistent tampering in some cases, but it can also degrade the model performance when the image is too low-quality and the frequency is too noisy.
elements.sent-2411-266: As a result, neither pure vision nor vision+frequency modeling can consistently provide the optimal solution.
elements.sent-2411-267: To achieve general IML through a flexible encoding modality, we propose the Modal Gate, which automatically determines the optimal encoding modality (frequency+vision or pure vision) for each input sample.
elements.sent-2411-268: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig: main , we extract vision features $F_{rgb}$ using Vision Perception Head, extract frequency features using Frequency Perception Head, and obtain the fused features $F_{fused}$ by fusing the vision and frequency features with a conv-layer.}
elements.sent-2411-269: Both the two perception heads consist of several conv-layers, with the same structure as those in the DTD model~\cite{CVPR2023DocTamper .}
elements.sent-2411-270: Two coarse mask predictions $P_{rgb}$ and $P_{fused}$ are obtained from $F_{rgb}$ and $F_{fused}$ with two conv-layers respectively.. Modal Gate.
elements.sent-2411-271: As shown in the left part of Figure~\ref{fig: Modules , $F_{rgb}$, $F_{fused}$, $P_{rgb}$ and $P_{fused}$ are channel-dimension concatenated and fed into the Modal Gate for optimal modality prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-272: The Modal Gate is a binary classifier consists of several conv-layers and activation layers to determine whether to use $F_{fused}$ or $F_{rgb}$ as the encoder input, {by observing the noise level and the confidence of $F_{fused}$, $F_{rgb}$ and their corresponding coarse predictions $P_{rgb}$ and $P_{fused}$.}
elements.sent-2411-273: More details are presented in the appendix.
elements.sent-2411-274: The Modal Gate Encoder maximizes the advantages of frequency domain modeling especially when the visual anomalies are limited (e.g. document images), and avoids its drawbacks when the image is too complex and noisy (e.g. natural images).

elements.sent-2411-283: After training, the $F_{a}$ and $F_{b}$ contain positive features enhanced by the detection supervision, we add them to the original features $F_{2}$ and $F_{3}$ and fuse them with conv-layer to get $F_{2'}$ and $F_{3'}$.

elements.sent-2411-285: With the AE module, the tampered regions in features $F_{2}$ and $F_{3}$ can be enhanced and the false-positive noise can be reduced.
elements.sent-2411-286: Different types of tampered images result in a wide range of manipulation clues.
elements.sent-2411-287: For example, forged objects in natural images may have abnormal contrast or edge artifact~\cite{wang2022objectformer , tampered text in document images might be visually consistent but has discontinuous BAG in frequency domain~\cite{CVPR2023DocTamper , fake faces may have unnatural texture~\cite{hifi_net_xiaoguo .}}}

elements.sent-2411-291: In the proposed Dynamic Weight Decoder, the low-level input features are fused with high-level input features by Pyramid Pooling Module ~\cite{zhao2017pyramid and Feature Pyramid Network~\cite{lin2017fpn to obtain multi-scale features $F_{1 , F_{2 , F_{3 , F_{4}$.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-295: As shown in the top-right of Figure~\ref{fig: Modules , to obtain the dynamic filters, we first average pool the input feature to obtain a current global representation $V_c$ (orange box), then interact $V_c$ with the global image vector $V_g$ (blue box) with a fully connected layer and identify the optimal dynamic filters $D_{opt}$ by weighted summation of four common convolutional filters.}

elements.sent-2411-298: To provide more convincing and reliable forgery analysis by describing the image artifact in natural language, we introduce the Interpretation Module (IM), as shown on the right of Figure~\ref{fig: main .}
elements.sent-2411-299: Existing works directly input the tampered image into a multimodal LLM to describe the image artifact.
elements.sent-2411-300: However, due to the challenging nature of image forensics, the LLM often misidentifies the tampered region especially on multi-target and challenge scenarios (e.g. tampered documents).
elements.sent-2411-301: To address this issue, we propose to draw LLM's attention to the suspect regions by presenting it with the forgery localization mask predicted by the decoder.
elements.sent-2411-302: However, directly inputting the binary mask predicted by LM will lead to considerable ambiguity in dense text and dense face images, since adjacent instances may have very similar positions.
elements.sent-2411-303: To minimize the ambiguity and the difficulty for IM to understand the predicted tampered regions, we construct a visual reference prompt $I_{ref}$ by highlighting the mask predictions $I_{mask}$ (normed to 0-255) on the tampered image $I_{input}$ by pixel-wise addition: $I_{ref =(I_{input +I_{mask )/2$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-304: We concatenate the $I_{ref}$ with $I_{input}$ along the longest side, and feed it into a multimodal LLM for text prediction.
elements.sent-2411-305: Besides clearly indicating the suspected region, our method can also minimize overfitting and forgetting for multimodal LLMs, since it does not change their original structure.
elements.sent-2411-306: To enable the Omni-IML for high-quality description of the tampered region, we construct the Omni-273k dataset, by querying GPT-4o to generate textual artifact descriptions for the tampered images in multiple major IML tasks.
elements.sent-2411-307: Existing works generate textual descriptions for all tampered objects' content, position and artifact clues in a forged image by querying with a single prompt~\cite{xu2024fakeshield, huang2024sida .}
elements.sent-2411-308: These methods make progress on single-target and less challenging scenarios such as natural object images, but do not work well on tampered documents, scene texts and uncut deepfakes where image artifacts are less obvious and a tampered image usually contains multiple tampered instances.
elements.sent-2411-309: There are two main reasons for this: First, given the challenging nature of image forensics, accurately describing the content, location, and artifact of a tampered instance requires focused analysis.
elements.sent-2411-310: Simultaneous analysis of multiple targets leads to distractions, causing the model to confuse the artifact among different objects.
elements.sent-2411-311: Second, for challenging samples such as forged documents, the response of GPT-4o is often unconfident and partially incorrect.
elements.sent-2411-312: These problems prevent previous methods from being an adequate solution for unified image forgery analysis.
elements.sent-2411-313: To solve the above problems and improve annotation quality in all major scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-314: We propose a novel chain-of-thoughts pipeline, which consists of three steps, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig: CoT : Step 1.}

elements.sent-2411-318: For text images, the OCR result for each tampered instance is the content, which is obtained through OCR engine.
elements.sent-2411-319: For other images, we highlight the $m$ tampered regions in $I_{tamp}$ respectively to get $m$ highlight masks $I^{n _{high}$, $n\in[1,m]$.}

elements.sent-2411-363: The previous IML methods suffer more performance degradation and show much worse average performance than our Omni-IML in joint training.
elements.sent-2411-364: For example, for TIFDM, its IoU on document IML is 0.568 when it's trained on document IML task only, but its IoU on document IML is 0.499 when it's trained on all the four IML tasks, which is a degradation of 6.9 points.

elements.sent-2411-380: The experiments on interpretation task are conducted on our Omni-273k dataset.
elements.sent-2411-381: In Table~\ref{tab: mllm1 , we directly evaluate models with their whole output string for each sample using word-vectors cosine similarity~\cite{mikolov2017cossim , ROUGE and BLEU, following the previous works~\cite{xu2024fakeshield, huang2024sida .}}}
elements.sent-2411-382: All the pre-trained models (the top group in Table~\ref{tab: mllm1 ) show poor interpretation performance (e.g. all BLEU scores less than 0.1), confirming that the pre-trained LLMs are not naturally adequate to detect and explain image forgery.}
elements.sent-2411-383: Fine-tuning the models with our dataset is necessary for them to achieve forgery interpretation capability.
elements.sent-2411-384: The models fine-tuned with our method (bottom group) consistently outperform those fine-tuned without our method (middle group) under all metrics, confirming the effectiveness of the reference visual prompt in our interpretation module.
elements.sent-2411-385: Directly evaluating models on the unstructured string format as in previous works~\cite{xu2024fakeshield, huang2024sida is coarse, as it can merely show the final result but cannot provide further insights behind the result.}
elements.sent-2411-386: Moreover, all the common metrics are insufficient: cosine similarity is indiscriminate and ROUGE, BLEU can not recognize synonyms.
elements.sent-2411-387: To provide better analysis, we convert the model output from JSON string to key-value pairs (Section 4.2) and perform fine-grained evaluation with them.
elements.sent-2411-388: As shown in Table~\ref{tab: mllm2 , we use OCR accuracy~\cite{zhang2019ic19 to evaluate the descriptions of tampered content and relative position for text images, and use Mean of ROUGE-L and BLEU (MRB) for non-text images.}}
elements.sent-2411-389: The description answers for absolute position (e.g. "Top left") and artifacts title (e.g. "Edge Artifacts") are close-ended, they can be regarded as single-choice and multi-choice tasks respectively, so we use accuracy to evaluate the ratio of matches.
elements.sent-2411-390: The detailed artifact description sentences are open-ended, and again we use the MRB score to evaluate them.
elements.sent-2411-391: By comparing the model fine-tuned with and without our method, we can learn that our visual prompt improves model by reducing the misidentification of tampered regions, especially in multi-target, challenging scenarios such as documents.
elements.sent-2411-392: For example, for the Qwen2.5-VL 7B fine-tuned without our method, the description scores for tampered text, absolute and relative positions are $.312, .381, .429$ respectively on documents.
elements.sent-2411-393: These low scores mean that the model often incorrectly detect the tampered regions.
elements.sent-2411-394: In contrast, the same model fine-tuned with our method gets the three much higher scores of $.647, .569, .692$ respectively.
elements.sent-2411-395: This means our visual prompt assists the model to correctly identify the tampered region, and consequently the artifact description is much more accurate (e.g. $.681$ outperforms .521 in artifact description accuracy).
elements.sent-2411-396: Similar conclusion to other image types such as scene texts and faces.
elements.sent-2411-397: Our method is evaluated on various state-of-the-art LLMs and consistently yields obvious improvements (e.g. +21\% average score for Intern-VL 2B and + 16\% for Qwen2.5-VL 7B), demonstrating high generality and effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-398: Model interpretation predictions are presented in the appendix.
elements.sent-2411-399: In this paper, we propose Omni-IML, the first generalist model for Image Manipulation Localization (IML).
elements.sent-2411-400: Specifically, our generalist model achieves generalizable localization through multiple novel and effective modules, including a Modal Gate Encoder, a Dynamic Weight Decoder, and an Anomaly Enhancement module.
elements.sent-2411-401: Further, to enable language interpretation for image artifact, we construct a large-scale, comprehensive, high-quality dataset Omni-273k.
elements.sent-2411-402: The textual annotations in this dataset are automatically curated by a novel chain-of-thoughts pipeline, which significantly improves annotation quality.
elements.sent-2411-403: A simple-yet-effective interpretation module is also introduced to better interpret the tampered regions.
elements.sent-2411-404: According to extensive experiments on four major IML tasks (natural IML, document IML, face IML, and scene text IML), our single model can achieve state-of-the-art performance simultaneously on all tasks.
elements.sent-2411-405: In-depth analysis are provided by our comprehensive ablation studies.
elements.sent-2411-406: We believe that our work can inspire future research and promote the real-world applications in unified image forensics.. \bibliographystyle{ACM-Reference-Format.}
